title.emphasis: Section 2: The Dichotomy of Regulation: California's Prohibition vs. Nevada's Formalization

section.para: The legal treatment of commercial sexual services in the United States is not monolithic; rather, it is characterized by a profound dichotomy, exemplified by the legal frameworks of California and Nevada. California represents the prevailing approach of absolute prohibition, where the law identifies the act of exchanging compensation for sex as a criminal offense, rendering any associated agreement void ab initio. Nevada, in stark contrast, has created a regulatory apparatus that, within specific and controlled circumstances, removes the taint of illegality and gives legal form to what can be understood as a new class of commercial agreement: the sexual service contract. Analyzing these two systems in parallel reveals that they are not merely different in degree, but different in kind. One is a system of criminal law aimed at suppressing an act; the other is a system of administrative and contract law aimed at regulating a service.

title.emphasis: 2.1. The Anatomy of a Prostitution Contract: An Unenforceable Agreement

section.para: In California, the legal framework is designed to ensure that any agreement for the exchange of sexual services for compensation is legally nonexistent and unenforceable. The cornerstone of this regime is California Penal Code § 647(b), which defines the crime of prostitution broadly to include not only the commission of a sexual act for compensation but also the acts of soliciting or agreeing to such an act.10 This is a critical feature of the law: the crime is completed at the moment of contract formation. The verbal or non-verbal exchange that constitutes an offer and acceptance is, in itself, the prohibited conduct. The law makes no distinction between a coercive arrangement and a consensual one; the presence of "money, goods or services" as consideration for a "lewd act" or sexual intercourse is the sole determinant of criminality.10

This statutory construction has profound implications under contract law. The common law doctrine of legality, a foundational principle of contract jurisprudence, asserts that agreements which breach statutory laws or violate public policy are inherently unenforceable.7 Courts will not lend their authority to enforce contracts that are founded on illegal acts, as doing so would undermine the integrity of the legal system and condone conduct that society, through its laws, has deemed harmful.8 A contract to commit a crime is the archetypal example of an agreement that is void against public policy.17

Because California Penal Code § 647(b) criminalizes the very formation of the agreement, a "prostitution contract" in California is void from its inception. It is not merely voidable at the option of one party; it is a legal nullity. Neither party can seek legal recourse for a breach. For example, a client who pays but does not receive the agreed-upon service cannot sue for restitution, nor can a service provider who performs but is not paid sue for the fee. The parties are considered to be in pari delicto—in equal fault—and the courts will leave them where they are found.18

The public policy undergirding this prohibition is broad and historically rooted in concepts of public morality and decency.19 The state's interest is not framed in the narrow, targeted terms of preventing specific harms like trafficking or disease, but as a wholesale rejection of the commercialization of sex as an act that is injurious to societal welfare and moral standards.7 This approach treats the agreement itself as the social harm to be eradicated. Consequently, the California model does not regulate sexual commerce; it attempts to eliminate it by rendering the foundational contractual element illegal, thereby creating a legal vacuum in which no rights, obligations, or protections can exist for the parties involved.

title.emphasis: 2.2. The Architecture of a Sexual Service Contract: A Regulated Agreement

section.para: In stark contrast to California's prohibitory model, Nevada has constructed a legal architecture that does not merely create an exception to prostitution laws but establishes an entirely different legal category: the regulated sexual service contract. This system operates by decriminalizing the act of exchanging sex for compensation under a strict set of conditions, thereby allowing for the formation of valid, albeit heavily regulated, commercial agreements. The Nevada framework is not an endorsement of "prostitution" as it is commonly understood; rather, it is a legislative recognition that, under certain controls, a contract for sexual services can be brought within the bounds of public policy.

The legality of these contracts is geographically and structurally circumscribed. Prostitution is only legal in licensed brothels, and these brothels are only permitted in counties with populations below a certain threshold (currently 700,000 residents), which excludes major urban centers like Las Vegas (Clark County) and Reno (Washoe County).11 This creates a "safe harbor" where the formation of a sexual service contract is not a criminal act and is therefore not automatically void against public policy.

Within this safe harbor, the state of Nevada imposes a comprehensive set of non-negotiable terms that are implicitly incorporated into every sexual service contract. These state-mandated terms function as a form of consumer and worker protection and public health regulation. Key provisions include:

listitem.para: Health and Safety Mandates: Workers must undergo weekly testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia and monthly testing for HIV and syphilis.11 Condom use is mandatory for most sexual acts.11 A positive test for certain infectious diseases requires the immediate cessation of work.22

Licensing and Registration: Both the brothel establishment and the individual sex worker must be licensed and registered with the county sheriff, which includes age verification to ensure all workers are adults (minimum age varies by county from 18 to 21).2

Operational and Advertising Restrictions: Brothels are subject to strict zoning laws, prohibiting them from operating near schools or places of worship or on principal streets.12 Furthermore, advertising is heavily restricted, particularly in counties where prostitution is illegal.12

section.para: This extensive regulatory scheme fundamentally transforms the nature of the transaction. It is no longer an illicit, hidden act but a formal, state-sanctioned commercial service. The state's role shifts from that of a punitive enforcer to a regulatory overseer. This legal distinction is analogous to the one between illegal prostitution and legal escort services, where the crucial difference lies in the intent and nature of the agreement; an escort contract is for companionship, while a prostitution contract is for sex.23 Nevada's framework effectively creates a new category—a contract for sexual services where the terms are predefined by the state to mitigate public policy concerns. By doing so, the state implicitly declares that a contract for sexual services is not

inherently contrary to public policy, but that an unregulated one is. The regulation itself is what redefines the act, moving it from the realm of criminal law into the domain of contract and administrative law.
The following table starkly illustrates the fundamental differences between the two legal constructs.

row.entry: Legal and Regulated (in licensed brothels in specific counties) 11
Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) & Nev. Administrative Code (NAC) 2
Exchange of compensation for a "lewd act" or sexual intercourse. 10
Formation of a contract for services within a licensed, regulated entity. 12
Independent Contractor (subject to some dispute and regulation) 2
Unregulated; possession of condoms historically used as evidence. 27
Mandatory weekly/monthly STI testing; mandatory condom use. 11
State/County Health Authorities; County Sheriff (licensing, registration). 2
Prohibition of the act to prevent moral/social harm. 7
Harm reduction, public health, and economic regulation. 2

section.para: This comparative analysis demonstrates that Nevada has not simply "legalized" the act that California prohibits. Instead, it has created the legal conditions for an entirely different type of transaction to exist: a formal, regulated, and enforceable sexual service contract, distinct in its legal character and public policy implications from the void and illegal prostitution contract.
